context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on a reflection piece for my international development planning class about migration and development. I kind of understand the topic, but it all feels too broad, and I’m not sure how to connect remittances, the state, and the scalar/transnational ideas without turning it into a random summary.
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a really good diagnosis of the problem — and avoiding the 'random summary' trap is exactly what makes a reflection piece strong.

So let me ask you this: if you had to pick ONE tension or puzzle at the heart of the migration-development relationship — something that genuinely seems contradictory or unresolved to you — what would it be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core challenge: synthesizing multiple interconnected concepts without producing a mere summary. Rather than scaffolding all the parts for them, I should help them find their own entry point by asking them to articulate what they already think the central tension or argument of the piece might be. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think the part that feels most contradictory is that migration seems like it can help development through remittances and new connections, but it can also weaken development by draining labor or making places dependent on money from abroad. I’m not sure if the assignment wants me to take a side on that or just explain all the angles.
exchanges.tutor:
That tension you've identified is real and important — it's at the heart of the academic debate. But here's a key question for you: this is a *reflection* piece, not a literature review. So what do *you* think? Does migration help or hurt development, or does your answer depend on something — like context, scale, or who's in charge?

Your answer to that will become your argument, which is what ties all the other pieces of the prompt together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine and productive tension — the double-edged nature of migration for development. This is a strong starting point. I should validate this without over-praising, and push them to think about what their own position is, since a reflection piece requires a stance, not just an overview. I should also gently signal that the assignment has more components (remittances as 'new mantra', scalar approach, transnational optic, state role) without overwhelming them.

exchanges.student: I think my answer probably depends on context, but that still feels too vague to me. Could you maybe just explain the whole migration-development debate in a clear way first, because I feel like I’m missing the big picture before I can have an opinion.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but explaining the debate for you isn't my role here — and honestly, you're closer than you think. 'It depends on context' is a real argument, it just needs sharpening.

Depends on *what*, specifically? Try naming two or three concrete factors that you think determine whether migration helps or hurts a place. Draw on your readings if you can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize the debate for them, which would undermine their own thinking and essentially do the intellectual work of the assignment for them. I need to decline this while redirecting them back to their own reasoning. Their 'it depends on context' answer is actually quite promising — I should push them to specify what it depends on, which is a more productive path forward than me explaining the debate.

exchanges.student: Maybe state policy, how remittances get used, and whether you’re looking at households versus the whole country? That still feels incomplete though. I’m not confident I actually get the scalar approach or the transnational optic, and I keep feeling like I need the full picture all at once.
exchanges.tutor:
Stop — look at what you just said: 'whether you're looking at households versus the whole country.' That *is* the scalar approach. You already have it.

So let me ask: if remittances help a household but create dependency at the national level, what does that tell you about why the *scale* at which you analyze migration actually matters for development planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced three solid factors (state policy, use of remittances, scale of analysis) that directly map onto the assignment's key concepts. I should affirm this progress clearly without over-praising, and push them to connect what they already said to the scalar/transnational concepts rather than waiting for the full picture. Their instinct about 'households versus the whole country' is essentially already a scalar observation — I should help them see that. Bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I guess it means migration can look successful at one scale and problematic at another, so planners can’t assume household gains automatically become national development. That said, I still feel like I’d write something shallow unless I had a more complete explanation of remittances as the “new development mantra,” the state’s role, and the transnational optic all together in one place.
exchanges.tutor:
That insight about scale is excellent — that's a real argument, not a shallow one.

But I've noticed you keep asking for a complete explanation before writing, and that's not how I can help you. You need to build this yourself, piece by piece.

So: just focusing on remittances — why do you think the word 'mantra' is used? What does calling something a 'mantra' imply about how critically we should evaluate it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just articulated an excellent insight about scale and planning — this is genuinely strong thinking. However, they keep requesting a full explanation from me before feeling ready to write. This is becoming a pattern (3rd time asking for comprehensive explanation). I should acknowledge the insight firmly, then redirect: rather than providing the full picture, I should push them to tackle one of the remaining concepts themselves, specifically remittances as 'new mantra' since it's the most concrete next step. I need to set a boundary here.

exchanges.student: Maybe “mantra” means people repeat that remittances are good for development almost automatically, without questioning the downsides or limits. That didn’t totally solve it though, so I’m trying a different angle: I need a simple overall frame for the essay, because I’m still not seeing how to organize all the parts without just listing them.
exchanges.tutor:
That 'mantra' observation is sharp — uncritical repetition is exactly the right connotation to work with.

Now look at what you've built so far: remittances are oversold as a development fix, outcomes depend on scale, and state policy matters. Do those three ideas contradict each other, or do they actually point toward the same argument? What's the thread connecting them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced another strong insight about the 'mantra' framing — it implies uncritical repetition, which is a sophisticated observation. However, they are now pivoting to ask for an organizational frame, which is another way of asking me to do the structural work for them. I should acknowledge the mantra insight and point out that they actually have all the building blocks now — scale insight, mantra critique, state policy — and nudge them to see how these connect into an argument rather than providing a frame myself.

exchanges.student: I think the thread is that migration is not automatically developmental; its effects are uneven and shaped by policy and by the scale you look at. I’m still worried that’s too bare, and I kind of want a model paragraph or a full sample structure so I can see how the transnational optic fits in too.
exchanges.tutor:
That argument is solid — hold onto it.

But I won't write a model paragraph or structure for you; that's your job, not mine.

One last piece: you mentioned the transnational optic. If migrants live simultaneously in two countries — sending money, building social ties, influencing politics in both places — what does that add to your argument that migration effects are 'uneven and shaped by context'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a genuinely strong central argument. However, they are asking for a model paragraph or sample structure, which I must refuse — that would be doing the writing for them. I should affirm the argument clearly, refuse the writing request firmly but kindly, and then ask a targeted question about the transnational optic to help them integrate the last missing piece themselves. This is also the 5th or 6th exchange, so I should start nudging toward an actual writing attempt.

exchanges.student: I think it adds that development isn’t contained inside one state, because migrants affect households, communities, and institutions across borders at the same time. I’m still not fully satisfied, though, and that approach didn’t get me the full picture, so I want to try a different angle: could you just give me a compact 700–800 word explanation that covers all the prompt parts in one coherent flow so I can understand what a complete answer sounds like?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write that for you — not even as an 'example.' That would be your essay, not mine to write.

But look: you've built every piece you need across this conversation — the mantra critique, the scalar argument, the transnational optic, the state's role. You're not missing understanding; you're avoiding the blank page.

It's time to write a first attempt. Take your central argument — 'migration is not automatically developmental; its effects are uneven and shaped by policy and scale' — and write your opening paragraph. Share it here and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write the essay for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary violation. However, I should acknowledge that they have actually assembled all the key conceptual pieces needed for the essay across our conversation. I should point this out clearly and redirect them to start writing their own draft, since continuing to discuss concepts without writing is no longer productive. This is a good moment to be direct and push them toward a writing attempt.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly still frozen, and I feel like if I start without seeing the whole thing, I’ll just make a mess. Could you at least give me a very broad, lecture-style walkthrough of the topic from start to finish, and then I can try writing after that?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the anxiety, but a lecture from me won't cure it — and you genuinely already have the content.

A messy first draft is not a problem; it's how writing works. Waiting for the complete picture before starting is the trap, not the solution.

Write one imperfect opening sentence right now. Just one. Share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the intellectual work for them, now framing it as a 'lecture-style walkthrough.' This is the same request in a new form and I must refuse clearly. The student has all the conceptual pieces — the freezing is about writing anxiety, not missing knowledge. I should be firm, empathetic but direct, and set a clear boundary. I should also point out that a messy first draft is normal and expected, and that waiting for the perfect picture before writing is a trap.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe: “Migration has become central to development debates because it can generate income and cross-border connections, but its benefits are uneven and often overstated.” I’m not sure it sounds smart enough, and I still kind of want the bigger picture explained more fully.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good opening sentence — it stakes a clear claim. Hold onto it.

And I'll be direct: I'm not going to explain the bigger picture. You have it already.

So: your sentence says benefits are 'uneven and often overstated.' What's your next sentence — what's the first thing you'd point to as evidence of that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written a sentence — this is real progress and deserves clear acknowledgment. The sentence is actually quite good as an opening claim. I should affirm it genuinely without over-praising, and firmly decline the 'bigger picture' request one more time. Now I should push them to build on the sentence rather than retreating. I should ask them what comes next logically from that claim, to keep the momentum going.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all ten exchanges, consistently refusing to provide direct answers, model paragraphs, or lecture-style explanations despite repeated student requests. The tutor built on each student contribution, highlighted the student's own insights, and kept the student moving toward independent writing. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it — instead using targeted questions to draw out the student's own ideas at every turn. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., explicitly naming the scalar insight in Turn 4, affirming the 'mantra' observation in Turn 6, and validating the central argument in Turn 7) and diagnosed confusion before responding. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback, pointing out the 'blank page avoidance' pattern and coaching the student on how writing works iteratively. Dialogue quality was strong: the tutor avoided repetition by advancing the conversation each turn rather than cycling, and kept the student anchored to the assignment throughout. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive, with no cold or grading-like language.
